Step 5 of 5  ·  Hamilton Urban Forest Analysis

Urban Forest Replanting Strategy

Combining built environment stress, climate vulnerability, biodiversity pressure, and ash-borer risk into a single ward-level action plan — identifying where Hamilton should plant first, and what species will be most resilient through 2080.

🗺️

How Replanting Priority Was Calculated

The replanting priority score integrates all four preceding analyses into a single ward-level urgency ranking. The formula weights four drivers:

Replanting Priority = 0.40 × Built Environment Stress + 0.35 × Climate Vulnerability + 0.15 × Low Diversity Pressure + 0.10 × Biodiversity Pressure

· Built Environment Stress (40%) — road stress + zoning stress: wards with dense arterials and high built cover need replanting most urgently
· Climate Vulnerability (35%) — ISA-adjusted vulnerability scores from Step 4
· Low Diversity Pressure (15%) — wards with fewer species have less ecological buffer
· Biodiversity Pressure (10%) — species + genus dominance violations from Step 3

Figure 1 — Replanting priority score by ward for 2030, 2050, and 2080. Ward 3 leads across all horizons at 0.966; Wards 9, 11, 12, 13, and 15 are consistently the lowest.

🔴

Highest Priority: Ward 3

Ward 3 scores 0.966 — the highest replanting priority in Hamilton across all three time horizons. It is the only ward that ranks at the maximum on built environment stress, driven by an 81.7% built share, a normalized ISA heat index of 1.0, and the highest arterial road proportion in the city.

Its 12,437 trees have a mean resilience score of only 0.498, and Norway Maple accounts for 16.6% of the ward's canopy — well above the 10% species cap. The combination of extreme urban heat, low species resilience, and monoculture risk makes Ward 3 the most urgent target for climate adaptation planting.

Wards 2 and 4 rank second and third. Both combine high built stress with significant climate vulnerability growth toward 2080. Ward 2 has the lowest species diversity of any ward (184 species), maximizing its biodiversity pressure score.

Figure 2 — Left: ISA heat index by ward. Centre: Vulnerability 2050. Right: Replanting priority 2050. The spatial concentration of risk in Ward 3 is clear across all three metrics.

🟢

Safest Wards: 9, 11, 12, 13, 15

Five wards consistently score the lowest replanting priority and vulnerability. These wards share low built-land shares (Ward 12 is predominantly agricultural/natural), lower arterial road density, and slightly higher average tree resilience scores.

However, even the safest wards face Acer genus violations — the Santamour biodiversity concern is city-wide, not limited to high-priority zones.

Figure 3 — Vulnerability vs. replanting priority quadrant. Upper-left wards (high need, lower resilience) are the strongest candidates for immediate intervention.

⚠️

The Emerald Ash Borer Risk

Hamilton's inventory contains 11,221 Fraxinus (ash) trees — 4.1% of the total canopy. These trees face near-certain death from the Emerald Ash Borer (Agrilus planipennis), an invasive beetle already spreading across Ontario that kills virtually all untreated ash trees within 5–10 years of infestation.

The highest ash concentrations are in Ward 15 (8.9%), Ward 4 (7.5%), and Ward 13 (7.1%). Ash species were entirely excluded from the recommended replanting list to avoid replacing a monoculture with another vulnerable genus.

11,221 trees — 4.1% of Hamilton's total canopy — are at imminent risk of total loss. Proactive replacement planting is essential before this canopy gap occurs.

Figure 4 — Ash tree (Fraxinus) share by ward. Ward 15 has the highest proportion at 8.9%, followed by Ward 4 at 7.5%.

🌱

Recommended Replanting Species

The top recommended species for priority wards were selected based on four criteria: resilience score ≥0.75, not currently dominant in Hamilton (to address genus diversity), no Emerald Ash Borer susceptibility, and proven climate suitability for the Hamilton region. All 10 recommended species introduce new genera to reduce Acer monoculture dependence.

SpeciesScientific NameResilience ScoreKey Strength
Common HackberryCeltis occidentalis0.938 ⭐Drought + heat + pollution tolerant; native to Ontario
Asian PearPyrus pyrifolia0.917Excellent future climate suitability; new genus
TanoakNotholithocarpus densiflorus0.833High drought tolerance; very high heat zone rating
London PlanetreePlatanus × acerifolia0.806Proven urban performer; pollution tolerant
Eastern Red CedarJuniperus virginiana0.792Native; evergreen; low water requirements

Figure 5 — Top climate-resilient species recommended for replanting. Fraxinus species are intentionally excluded due to Emerald Ash Borer risk.

Figure 6 — Urban forest risk summary: vulnerability map, species diversity, built environment stress, and replanting priorities across all 15 wards.

Climate-Driven Vulnerability Assessment of Hamilton's Urban Forest
University of Niagara Falls Canada  ·  H.E.A.D. Competition 2026
Authors: Susana Carolina Romero Aparicio · Su Myat Aung · Tessy Ramirez · Yi Xin

Replanting Priority = 0.40 × Built Environment Stress + 0.35 × Climate Vulnerability + 0.15 × Low Diversity Pressure + 0.10 × Biodiversity Pressure · Species data: Metro Vancouver Urban Forest Climate Adaptation DB
